channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Push for Electoral Retribution Heads to the Ballot Box
item.description: The president’s push to punish political enemies in his own party will play a starring role in a series of Republican primaries this month in which he has backed challengers.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:description: President Donald Trump is seeking to unseat a raft of elected Republicans he perceives as political enemies in a series of primaries this month.

item.title: These G.O.P. Women Want Congress Members to Pay a Price for Abuse. Will It Work?
item.description: Three Republican women who had a hand in forcing the resignations of lawmakers accused of sexual misconduct said they are out to name and shame more offenders. It is not clear how far they will go.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representatives Nancy Mace, Anna Paulina Luna and Lauren Boebert.

item.title: Eric Swalwell Was Proficient in Social Media. So Were His Accusers.
item.description: Eric Swalwell used social media to boost his image and meet women, accusers said. Some of those same women used apps and influencers to join forces years later.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:description: Eric Swalwell in 2019, during a brief run for president. Last month, he left office after sexual misconduct allegations.

item.title: U.S. to Withdraw 5,000 Troops From Germany, Pentagon Says
item.description: Officials announced the decision after President Trump expressed annoyance with the German chancellor’s remarks about the Iran war.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Helene Cooper
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Members of the Army participated in a military exercise in Hohenfels, Germany, on Thursday.

item.title: How Germany May Have Misjudged Trump’s Anger on Iran
item.description: After Chancellor Friedrich Merz upset President Trump with criticism of the war, he offered no public sign he believed Mr. Trump’s threats to pull troops were serious.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump speaking with Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany in the Oval Office in March.

item.title: Hegseth’s and Moulton’s Parallel Lives Collide Over Iran
item.description: The defense secretary and Representative Seth Moulton, a Democrat, debated the Iran war through the prism of their shared experiences fighting Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth during a news conference at the Pentagon last month.

item.title: Not Even Japanese Bathhouses Are Immune From Shocks of Iran War
item.description: High fuel costs are imperiling the Japanese sento, a long-declining industry that remains a vital social lifeline for the country’s isolated elderly.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis, Kiuko Notoya and Kentaro Takahashi

item.title: Spirit Airlines Shuts Down After Years of Struggle
item.description: Spirit once upended the industry by offering very low fares but was in its second bankruptcy in two years after years of struggle.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines was such a disruptive force in its industry that other airlines felt compelled to lower their fares when it announced that it would start flying to one of their airports.

item.title: Spirit Airlines is Shutting Down. Here’s What to Do if You’re Scheduled to Fly.
item.description: After two stints in bankruptcy, the low-cost airline shut down early Saturday. Here’s what travelers should know about rebooking and refunds.
item.dc:creator: Seth Kugel
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines, squeezed by spiking jet fuel prices, is aiming to shut down after a proposed government bailout fell apart.

item.title: Trump’s Vision for D.C. ‘Garden of Heroes’ Statues Grows in Size and Cost
item.description: Plans for the project include formal gardens, reflecting pools and plazas alongside the statues of 250 notable Americans, according to documents obtained by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater and Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Based on the latest renderings, the Garden of Heroes could rank among the more expensive and time-consuming projects President Trump has undertaken.

item.title: Kennedy Consulted With Scandal P.R. Firm While Seeking Cabinet Nomination
item.description: An associate of the firm, which has been accused of running a smear campaign against the actress Blake Lively, promised to suppress negative stories about Robert F. Kennedy Jr., records show.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin and Santul Nerkar
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. suspended his presidential campaign in August 2024, and President Trump later tapped him to be health secretary.

item.title: N.I.H. Reinstates Employee Put on Leave After Criticizing Trump Research Cuts
item.description: Jenna Norton had filed a whistle-blower complaint claiming that the agency leadership had retaliated against her.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:description: Jenna Norton in Bethesda, Md., last year.

item.title: Tucker Carlson Discusses Breaking With Trump, the Iran War and Antisemitism
item.description: The conservative media commentator split with the administration over the war in Iran. Will the breakup last?
item.dc:creator: Lulu Garcia-Navarro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity

item.title: Takeaways From The Times’s Interview With Tucker Carlson
item.description: He spoke about the rupture with President Trump and what it portends for the conservative movement. Here are the key moments from our conversation.
item.dc:creator: Lulu Garcia-Navarro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Philip Montgomery for The New York Times

item.title: How We Chose What to Ask and When to Challenge Tucker Carlson
item.description: “The Interview” host Lulu Garcia-Navarro talks about her preparation to interview Mr. Carlson and some judgment calls she had to make in real time.
item.dc:creator: Patrick Healy
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Lulu Garcia-Navarro spoke twice with Tucker Carlson for “The Interview,” a New York Times show.

item.title: Can a Second-Home Tax Work in New York? The Numbers Don’t Add Up Yet.
item.description: The tax proposal is being held up as a generational attempt to make the ultrawealthy pay more to society and potentially raise $500 million a year for New York City.
item.dc:creator: Mihir Zaveri and Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Very few condos or co-ops in New York City have an assessed value exceeding or even approaching $5 million.

item.title: Do Higher Taxes Drive the Rich Away? Mamdani’s Plan Revives the Question.
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani wants to raise income taxes on New York City’s millionaires. Tax increases elsewhere have not led to an exodus, but some worry the city is reaching a tipping point.
item.dc:creator: Emma G. Fitzsimmons and Asmaa Elkeurti

item.title: Ashley McBryde Saved Her Own Life. Now She’s Rocking Out Her Way.
item.description: The prolific country singer and songwriter has never quite fit the country music mold. Her upcoming album, “Wild,” revels in her individuality, and her sobriety.
item.dc:creator: David Peisner and Meghan Marin
item.media:description: “What made me have to stop drinking was the possibility of self-harm,” McBryde said. “After losing my brother to suicide, I was so scared that that’s somehow genetic.”

item.title: His Majesty and Our Travesty
item.description: The real king delivers a needed royal lesson on democracy.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Dowd
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom

item.title: Slouching Toward Kamala Harris
item.description: The Democrats’ fundamental condition is a late-Trumpian stasis.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat

item.title: Public Offering
item.description: How often do you talk to strangers? What’s stopping you?
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: Mexican Governor Accused by U.S. of Aiding Drug Cartel Steps Down
item.description: U.S. prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya of protecting the powerful Sinaloa Cartel in exchange for bribes and political support, setting off a national scandal.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of the Mexican state of Sinaloa, said he had decided to take “a temporary leave of absence.”

item.title: After Trump’s Ousting of Maduro, Little Has Changed for Most Venezuelans
item.description: U.S. officials say they will “unleash prosperity” by commandeering the oil industry. Many people in Caracas say it will take far more than that.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: People ride on a crowded bus during rush hour in Caricuao, Venezuela. A one-way bus ticket costs around $0.15 — half of the nation’s hourly minimum wage.

item.title: UK Driving-Test Backlog Leaves Learners Waiting Months
item.description: The average wait for a test in England and Scotland has hit 22 weeks, up from five weeks before the pandemic, leading to frustration and attempts to jump the line.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:credit: Andrew Testa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Grace Reynolds, right, taking a driving lesson with Dean Batchelor in March in London. “It feels like I’ve got to pass — otherwise Christmas will be probably the next time,” Ms. Reynolds said.
